verification_method: fresh independent re-judgment; did NOT read 07/06.5/06.7
candidate_summary: Behistun trilingual 3-of-3 blind agreement termination criterion for multi-tool LLM cascades

results.title: The Evolution of Tool Use in LLM Agents: From Single-Tool Call to Multi-Tool Orchestration
results.reason: Discusses multi-tool orchestration and cascading errors but no 3-of-3 blind agreement termination criterion. No exact content_word substring matches.

results.title: Chain-of-Tools (CoTools)
results.reason: Chain-of-tools is mentioned in candidate's novelty_claim as prior art that is NOT the Behistun mechanism. Tool selection cascade lacks blind multi-agent verification.

results.title: Robust and Efficient Tool Orchestration via Layered Execution Structures with Reflective Correction
results.reason: Layered + reflective correction is self-correction within a single chain, not 3-of-3 cross-language blind verification.

results.title: ToolRLA: Multiplicative Reward Decomposition for Tool-Integrated Agents
results.reason: Reward decomposition for RL training of tool agents; unrelated to multi-tool agreement termination.

results.title: Multi-Agent Debate for LLM Judges with Adaptive Stability Detection
results.reason: 3-agent CollabEval: independent assessment + consensus check + terminate on agreement. Functionally equivalent to Behistun 3-of-3 blind verification. Judge>=0.7 triggers hit. However, candidate's novelty_claim already flags multi-agent debate as known prior art (could be considered same as existing literature).

results.title: Improving Factuality and Reasoning with Multiagent Debate
results.reason: Foundational multi-agent debate paper: multiple LLM instances debate to consensus. Consensus-based termination functionally analogous to 3-of-3 agreement. Judge==0.70 just meets threshold; candidate explicitly cites multi-agent debate as the open question of functional identity.

results.title: Adaptive heterogeneous multi-agent debate
results.reason: Heterogeneous debate with adaptive agreement detection for termination. Heterogeneity (multiple different agents) + agreement termination = functional analog of trilingual cross-check.

results.title: Multi-Agent Debate for LLM Judges with Adaptive Stability Detection (arxiv)
results.reason: Same paper as index 4 (arxiv version). Same scoring.

disagreement_details.note: Primary judged Layered Execution + Reflective Correction as functional analog (judge=0.72). I scored it lower (0.4) because reflective correction is intra-chain self-critique, not multi-agent cross-language blind verification. Borderline — primary's 0.72 just barely crosses, mine just below. Minor scoring disagreement on a single result.

agreement_summary: Both verdicts: FAIL. Both agree on the 4 multi-agent debate results being hits (indices 4, 5, 6, 7). Both agree on indices 0, 1, 3 being misses. Single disagreement on index 2 (Layered Execution paper) — primary forces a functional hit, I do not. Total: primary 5 hits, independent 4 hits. Both pass the FAIL threshold easily.
honest_note: Independent verdict: FAIL (candidate is NOT novel) — agrees with primary. The 4 multi-agent debate papers (esp. arxiv 2510.12697 / OpenReview Vusd1Hw2D9 'Multi-Agent Debate for LLM Judges with Adaptive Stability Detection') are functional analogs of the proposed Behistun 3-of-3 blind agreement termination criterion. The candidate's own novelty_claim acknowledged this risk: 'Whether it is functionally identical to existing multi-agent debate / self-consistency is the open question' — evidence answers yes. Disagreement on index 2 is a minor judge-score difference on a borderline paper; verdicts coincide. Note: I assigned 0 keyword overlaps where the primary found 1-2 (e.g., 'multi-tool agent', 'self-consistency', 'embedding agreement'). The primary's substring matches appear plausible if titles/snippets contained those exact phrases via expanded retrieval text, but in the snippets shown here I did not see those exact strings literally. This is a recall/expansion difference, not an outcome difference.
